purpose:
To conduct an ablation study validating the impact of SMOTE and feature engineering, and to build a final Stacking Classifier ensemble.
The notebook explores how different data treatments affect performance and tests varying probability thresholds on the stacked model to fine-tune Recall.
key_steps: Conduct an ablation study by training a baseline Logistic Regression model on four distinct data states: Raw Data, Raw + SMOTE, Feature Engineering, and Feature + SMOTE.
Evaluate and export the results of the ablation study to quantify the value added by each data processing step.
Build a Stacking Classifier using Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM as base estimators, and Logistic Regression as the final meta-estimator.
Train and evaluate the Stacking Classifier, generating a confusion matrix.
Iterate through probability thresholds (0.1 to 0.5) to plot a Threshold vs. Recall curve for the stacking model.

images.06_ablation_hybrid_threshold_colab_phase: A confusion matrix for the final Stacking Classifier, showing its performance on the test set.
A line graph illustrating the inverse relationship between the decision threshold and the model's Recall score, demonstrating how lower thresholds capture more churners.

key_findings: The ablation study conclusively proves that SMOTE is the primary driver for improving Recall, jumping from ~49.1% (Raw) to ~65.8% (Raw + SMOTE).
Feature engineering alone actually slightly depressed Recall compared to Raw Data, but improved the structural learning for complex models in earlier phases.
The Threshold-Recall curve visualizes the trade-off space, confirming the findings from Phase 2's cost-sensitive notebook that lowering the threshold is necessary to maximize churn capture.
notes: This final notebook nicely ties together the methodological choices made throughout the project. The ablation study scientifically validates the necessity of the SMOTE step introduced in Phase 1A.
